facts_disclosed: Mom runs izakaya Nakamura-ya in Namba

turns.content: random question but do you know anything about menu design for restaurants? i need to redo one and i want to make sure i'm not missing any best practices

Great topic — menu design is one of those areas where good design directly impacts revenue. Here are the key best practices:

**Layout & Structure:**
- The upper-right area of a menu gets the most eye attention (sometimes called the "sweet spot") — put your highest-margin items there
- Group items into clear categories with distinct headers
- Don't number the items — it makes the menu feel institutional
- Keep descriptions short but evocative (2 lines max per item)

**Typography:**
- Use no more than 2-3 typefaces — one for headers, one for item names, one for descriptions
- Item names should be larger/bolder than descriptions
- Make sure there's enough contrast for dim restaurant lighting

**Pricing Psychology:**
- Drop the currency symbol (¥) — studies show it reduces the "pain of paying"
- Don't align prices in a column on the right — it encourages price comparison. Instead, tuck the price at the end of the description line
- Avoid dotted lines connecting item names to prices

**Practical:**
- Choose paper/coating that handles grease and moisture
- Test the font size under the restaurant's actual lighting

What kind of restaurant is this for? The style can vary a lot depending on the cuisine and vibe.

it's for an izakaya actually, so it needs to feel warm and inviting but not too fussy. like the food is traditional but the atmosphere is casual, people drinking beer and sharing plates at the counter kind of place. it's my mom's restaurant so i want to get it right lol

Ah, designing for family — the highest-stakes client with the lowest budget, right? No pressure at all.

For an izakaya specifically, here's how I'd approach it:

**Vibe:**
- Lean into the warmth — consider a kraft paper or textured stock that feels tactile and casual
- Handwritten-style Japanese typefaces can work beautifully here. Not literal handwriting, but fonts like Toppan Bunkyu Midashi Mincho or something with brush stroke character
- Warm color palette — think charcoal, deep red/vermillion, cream. Avoid anything that feels corporate or sterile

**Structure for izakaya style:**
- Organize by type: yakitori, sashimi, agemono, salads, rice dishes, etc.
- Include a "recommended" or seasonal section at the top — izakaya regulars love knowing what's good today
- If she has drink pairings or osusume combinations, highlight those

**Bilingual consideration:**
- If she gets any tourist traffic, a clean bilingual layout (Japanese primary, English secondary in smaller type) can help without cluttering

How big is the menu? Some izakayas have massive menus with 80+ items, others keep it tight. That changes the design approach significantly.

it's on the bigger side, probably 60-70 items. she's been running the place for close to twenty years now down in namba so the menu has kind of grown organically over time. Nakamura-ya — like, named after us, very original lol. the current menu is honestly just a laminated printout that she made in word or something and it's been bugging me for years that i haven't fixed it

Twenty years in Namba is no joke — that's real staying power in the restaurant business, especially in that area. The laminated Word printout has clearly been working for her, but yeah, a proper design would elevate the whole experience.

With 60-70 items, the biggest risk is overwhelming the customer. Here's how to handle that volume:

**Organization strategy:**
- Break it into a multi-page format — a single large foldout with 70 items will be chaos
- Consider a booklet-style menu (A5 or B5 size) with 4-6 pages. It gives you room to breathe
- Alternatively: a main menu with the core items + a separate insert card for seasonal/rotating specials. This also lets her swap out seasonal items without reprinting the whole thing

**Visual hierarchy for large menus:**
- Use subtle background color blocks or divider lines to separate categories
- Bold the 8-10 signature dishes or customer favorites — give people an anchor in the sea of options
- Small icons or stamps for things like "popular," "spicy," or "chef's pick" can guide decisions without adding text

**Practical tip for 20 years of organic growth:**
- Before designing, sit down with your mom and ask her which items she'd cut if she had to trim to 40. She might not actually cut them, but it'll reveal what she considers essential vs. legacy items. That hierarchy should inform the design.

Are you planning to do any food photography for it, or keep it text-only?

the seasonal insert card idea is brilliant actually, she changes specials like every month and reprinting is exactly why the last design attempt never happened. for photography i was thinking maybe a few hero shots of the signature stuff — her yakitori is honestly the best in osaka, i'm biased obviously but people line up for it — and then keep the rest text-only to avoid it looking like a chain restaurant

Exactly the right instinct — a few hero shots for the signatures, text-only for the rest. Chain restaurants plaster every item with a photo. A good izakaya lets the food speak for itself, with just enough photography to set expectations for first-timers.

For the hero shots:

- **3-5 photos max** throughout the whole menu. More than that and you lose the premium feel
- Shoot under the restaurant's actual lighting if possible, or warm artificial light that matches. Cold studio lighting will feel wrong for an izakaya
- Yakitori specifically photographs beautifully — the char marks, the glaze, the smoke. If you can capture them on the actual counter or grill, that's gold
- Shoot overhead or at a slight angle for shared plates; close-up and slightly angled for individual items like yakitori skewers

For the print production:
- **Main menu**: booklet, decent paper stock, matte or soft-touch finish. Print a batch of 30-50
- **Seasonal insert**: simpler stock, could even be a nice card printed at a local print shop. Easy to update monthly
- Get the insert card size to fit neatly inside the booklet — maybe a half-page tuck-in

Are you handling the photography yourself, or bringing someone in?

i'll probably do it myself, i have a decent camera and i know the food well enough to style it. growing up eating there every day has its perks i guess. plus hiring a food photographer would cost more than my mom would want to spend, she keeps telling me not to go overboard

The "don't go overboard" from a parent is universal — she'd probably be happy with a nicer laminated printout and here you are planning a booklet with hero photography. But she'll love the result.

Since you're shooting it yourself:

- **Best time to shoot**: before service starts, when the kitchen is prepping but the lighting is set for evening. You get the ambiance without the chaos
- **Bring a tripod** — izakaya lighting is warm but usually dim, so you'll need longer exposures or a wider aperture
- **Use actual plates and counter from the restaurant** — don't try to stage it somewhere else. The worn wood counter, the ceramic dishes she actually uses, that's the authenticity
- **Shoot extra** — take way more than you need. For 3-5 hero shots in the menu, shoot 40-50 photos across 8-10 dishes. You want options

For post-processing, keep it minimal — bump the warmth slightly, increase contrast a touch, but don't over-saturate. The food should look like it does when it actually lands in front of you.

What's the timeline on this? Is it something she needs soon, or more of a "when you get to it" project?

it's been a "when you get to it" project for approximately three years lol. but i told her i'd have something to show her by end of december so i actually need to get moving. i want to have the layout done and at least do a test print before new year

Three years of procrastination and now a December deadline — the classic creative timeline. At least the pressure is real now.

Here's a realistic production schedule to hit end-of-December:

**Week 1 (now):**
- Sit down with your mom, finalize the item list and categories
- Decide which 8-10 dishes to bold as signatures
- Photograph the hero dishes

**Week 2:**
- Design the layout — grid, typography, color palette
- Place all the content, rough draft
- Get feedback from your mom (budget for at least two rounds of "actually can we move this")

**Week 3:**
- Finalize layout, drop in hero photos
- Design the seasonal insert template
- Test print at home or local print shop to check font size, paper feel, photo quality

**Week 4 (Christmas week):**
- Final revisions, send to print
- Pick up printed menus, deliver to the restaurant

That's tight but doable for someone who does this professionally. The photography day is the main bottleneck — everything else flows from having the content and images ready.

Are you designing this in Figma too, or switching to InDesign for the print layout?

figma honestly. i know indesign is the "proper" tool for print layout but i'm so much faster in figma at this point. i'll just make sure to set it up at the right print dimensions and export as pdf for the printer. i've done a few packaging projects this way and it works fine

Totally fair — speed matters, especially on a tight timeline. Figma handles print dimensions just fine as long as you're mindful of a few things:

- **Set up frames at exact print size plus bleed** (usually 3mm on each side). So if the menu page is A5, your frame should be 154mm x 216mm instead of 148 x 210
- **Use a grid system** — for 60-70 items across 4-6 pages, consistent margins and gutters will keep everything from looking cramped
- **Export at 300 DPI** — Figma's default export is 72 DPI which is fine for screen but will look fuzzy in print. Use the "Export" settings to set the scale to 4x for raster elements, or export as PDF which handles vectors natively
- **Embed fonts or outline text** in the final PDF, especially if you're using any typefaces the print shop might not have

One InDesign advantage you'd miss is automatic text flow — with 70 items, placing and adjusting text in Figma is more manual. But if you've got a good component/auto-layout system built up, it's manageable.

This is going to be a really satisfying project to finish. Three years of that laminated printout staring at you every time you visit is finally getting resolved.

the 300 dpi reminder is clutch, i definitely would have forgotten that. okay yeah i'm feeling good about this, i've got a clear plan now. i'm going to head over to the restaurant this weekend to talk through the item list with my mom and probably eat way too much yakitori in the process. honestly the best part of this project. thanks for all the help

Research that involves eating your mom's yakitori is the kind of project expense that doesn't need a line item on the invoice.

Quick summary of your plan:

- **Booklet format** (A5 or B5), 4-6 pages, warm and casual feel
- **Separate seasonal insert card** that tucks inside — easy monthly swaps, no reprinting the whole menu
- **3-5 hero photos** of the signatures (yakitori front and center), text-only for everything else
- **Bold the house favorites**, use subtle category dividers, warm color palette
- **Shoot the food yourself** before service, real plates, real counter, real lighting
- **Figma layout** at print dimensions with 3mm bleed, export at 300 DPI
- **Week-by-week schedule** to hit end-of-December delivery

Start with the grid and type hierarchy before placing content — with this many items, the structure needs to be rock solid before you start filling it in.

Good luck, and enjoy the weekend visit. Twenty years of Nakamura-ya deserves a proper menu.
